notice: Rule corpus derived from the MIT-licensed gitleaks ruleset (see THIRD-PARTY-NOTICES.md). Rules are data, not code (R5).

rules.description: AWS secret access key. The separator alternation carries \\" (backslash-quote) because the scanner reads the wire body, not the decoded chat message: a pasted KEY="value" .env line arrives JSON-encoded as KEY=\"value\", so the quote's escape sits between the keyword and the secret. A raw body already matched that same paste on the bare quote, so without this the verdict depended on transport alone — the quoted form fell to the quiet tier and never alerted while the unquoted form did. ONLY backslash-quote is accepted, and only as a unit. Deliberately excluded, each measured to cost real precision on this known-FP-source rule: a bare \\ in the class (fires on Windows/UNC paths like aws\<40-hex-digest>), \\? before any separator (fires on a dir listing, aws\ <40-hex-digest>, and on aws\=<digest>), and \\[ntr] for escaped whitespace (swallows a path segment's first letter, firing on aws\node<39-more>). The last would have covered tab- and newline-separated pastes; those stay uncovered on purpose. The value and tail now match aws-secret-shape exactly. \\b needed a word char, so a real key ending in + or / was missed (~3% of the keyspace) and a longer run could match on its first 40 chars — which redact-on-capture would splice out, leaving the tail of a live token in the stored body. '=' is not a value character: 40 base64 chars encode exactly 30 bytes, so a genuine key never carries padding, and admitting it made every padded base64 blob near an aws keyword (data-URI images especially) fire once the tail stopped requiring a word char. HYPHENATED NAMES: the optional literal '-key' after the keyword window admits the canonical GitHub Actions spelling 'aws-secret-access-key:'. The window is [a-zA-Z_ ] with no hyphen, so once secret[_-]?access consumed 'secret-access' the trailing '-key' was uncrossable: the hyphenated form fell to the quiet tier and never alerted while the underscore form did, so the verdict turned on spelling alone. It also revives the 'secret-access' prescan keyword, which until now advertised a form the regex structurally could not reach. Two looser shapes were measured and rejected on an 896-case benign sweep (aws-cdk-lib / aws-sdk / aws-actions / ARN / Terraform identifiers x separators x 40-char blobs): widening the window to [a-zA-Z_ -] costs 75% FP, firing on any hyphen-chained identifier before a digest (aws-cdk-lib-construct-x <40-hex>, whose ~4.0 entropy clears the 3.0 gate); allowing bounded hyphen segments before the anchor, (?:-[a-zA-Z]{1,12}){0,3}-key, costs 31% FP by admitting aws-object-key, aws-partition-key and aws-sort-key - DynamoDB/S3 vocabulary where a trailing digest is ordinary. The literal anchor adds essentially no new false-positive class: nearly every spelling it newly accepts is the hyphen form of one the rule already accepts with underscores (aws_cache_key: <digest> alerts today). One asymmetry, measured and accepted: '-key' sits OUTSIDE the {0,20} window, so with 17-20 characters of filler the hyphen form reaches four characters further than its underscore sibling - aws_<20 chars>-key matches where aws_<20 chars>_key does not. The shape is unchanged (an aws-prefixed name ending in -key before a 40-char high-entropy run) and the band is 4 characters wide, but the symmetry argument above does not cover it, so it is stated rather than implied. 'aws-secret-key' - no 'access' - stays deliberately uncovered, since reaching it needs secret[_-]?key in the keyword alternation, which would pull the non-AWS SECRET_KEY family (Django) into an AWS-labelled rule. Entropy is 4.0 for the same digests-can't-reach-it reason aws-secret-shape gives, at the lowest setting that works: 40 chars over hex's 16 symbols is maximally even at 8x3 + 8x2, so a 40-char hex digest tops out at 3.9710 — a counting ceiling, not a sampled one, and it binds either case (uppercase hex is the same 16 symbols). Only a MIXED-case digest would draw from 22 symbols and could clear 4.0; no tool emits one, and the alternative fix (an all-hex lookahead) buys that corner at the price of an interior lookaround on a rule with a scan budget. At 3.0 this rule called a git SHA near an aws keyword a high-severity leak, bounded only by accident of phrasing ('aws deploy commit ' fits the 20-char window, 'aws deploy triggered by commit ' does not) — and the -key anchor widened that surface, since the hyphenated spelling now reaches a digest too. It does NOT borrow the sibling's 4.5: that rule is anchor-free and must be paranoid, and pays ~2% of real keys for it; this one has keyword context, so it sits just above the hex ceiling and keeps that recall — 0 of 2M synthetic real-shaped keys (base64 of 30 random bytes) fall under 4.0, observed floor 4.025, where 4.5 drops 1.81%.
GitHub personal access token (classic or fine-grained)
Mailgun API key (legacy key- format has no distinguishing shape beyond 32 hex, so require a mailgun context keyword or any key--prefixed md5/cache id would fire)
Telegram bot token (no anchor keyword exists for this shape; the :AA secret-part prefix is required to keep 'epoch:hash' pairs from firing)
Database/queue connection string with embedded password

Generic secret assignment (entropy-gated; quiet tier only; vendor test-mode keys and Twilio Key resource SIDs excluded). Twilio's SK + 32-lowercase-hex value is the API Key SID — an identifier paired with a separate secret — so the exact, whole-value SID shape (the shape the removed twilio-api-key rule matched) is not credential material. A value that is ENTIRELY a lowercase dotted identifier path ([a-z0-9_-] segments joined by dots, ending at a non-value char) is suppressed: documentation that DESCRIBES config keys is a key: "name" farm whose dotted names routinely clear the entropy gate (a fetched Codex config-reference page fired this rule nine times on names like memories.disable_on_external_context), while real key material essentially always carries case mixing or symbols outside [a-z0-9_-.]. The guards sit in (?-i:…) groups where case matters because the rule is i-flagged: a Twilio SID has an uppercase SK prefix over a lowercase-hex body, while a case-folded 'lowercase dotted path' would swallow every dotted token — a JWT's three base64url segments are exactly dot-joined [A-Za-z0-9_-] runs. Each guard is prefixed with =* because '=' is the one character shared by the key-separator class and the value class: the greedy separator can donate a trailing '=' into the value by backtracking (key =SK…, key:=SK…), shifting the value start off the guarded shape — skipping the leading '=' run keeps the guard aligned. The tail lookahead keeps each guard whole-value: a dotted lowercase PREFIX with secret material after it (prod.api.zQ3k…) still matches, because any parse of the identifier shape ends at a value-class char and the inner lookahead fails. Versions (1.2.3-alpha.4) and bare hostnames fall out of the same shape, deliberately: names, not secrets.

Anchor-free 40-char AWS-secret-shaped token (quiet tier). Entropy 4.5 is above pure hex's 4.0 ceiling, so digests can never trip it — the cost is ~2% of genuinely random real keys landing under the gate and being missed. Exact-length delimiters keep longer base64 runs (images, signatures) out; '=' is a valid leading delimiter (VAR=secret is the most common leak shape). Leading delimiter is consumed, not a lookbehind — 7x faster on a 1 MB body (R5 scan budget). The delimiter classes exclude '-' and '_' because the exact-length defense above holds only for STANDARD base64: in base64url those two are VALUE characters, but they are absent from [A-Za-z0-9/+], so without this they read as delimiters and chop one long blob into a stream of 40-char candidates — roughly 2 chars in 64, so a window lands on the boundary every few hundred characters and eventually one clears 4.5. Measured on live traffic: an OpenAI Responses API `encrypted_content` reasoning payload (provider-generated ciphertext the client resends each turn, 972–2232 chars) produced 7 findings across 165 real request bodies, every one a mid-blob slice with '-' or '_' on both sides, and 0 after. A synthetic 4000-char base64url blob containing no secret yields 1 before, 0 after. Recall is unchanged on the shapes that carry a real key — 2000 random base64-of-30-bytes keys across 10 contexts (env assignment, JSON value, JSON-escaped, YAML, CLI flag, URL query, bare line, prose, dotenv, shell export) move 0 detections, since a key is delimited by a quote, '=', whitespace or a line end, never by a base64url value char. What it deliberately gives up is a key ABUTTING one of those two characters (KEY_<40>, <40>-old): ~98% to 0% on that shape. Accepted — it is not a shape credentials are written in, and every keyword-bearing context (AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=, aws_secret_access_key:, export …) is matched by the structured-tier aws-secret-access-key rule instead, which this change does not touch.

Base64 blob that decodes to a structured secret (quiet tier). The decode-and-rescan validator makes this precise: base64 of anything else stays silent. Consumed leading delimiter (not lookbehind) and the entropy pre-gate keep the R5 scan budget: low-entropy runs never reach the decode probe.
